questions.text: In ITIL 4, which practice is responsible for ensuring that IT services and other business processes are delivered in accordance with agreed schedules and quality standards?
options: D. Risk Management
B. Incident Management
A. Service Level Management
C. Change Management
questions.justification_correct: Service Level Management defines, monitors, and reports on service quality agreements between IT and customers. It ensures alignment between service delivery and business expectations, maintaining agreed performance standards and schedules per ITIL 4 framework.
questions.justification_wrong: Incident Management addresses service disruptions, Change Management controls modifications, and Risk Management identifies threats. None specifically focus on monitoring and reporting against agreed service quality standards.

questions.text: Which practice within ITIL 4 is most appropriate for identifying, analyzing, and controlling risks that could impact IT services and organizational objectives?
options: C. Security Management
D. Supplier Management
B. Risk Management
A. Problem Management
questions.justification_correct: Risk Management identifies, analyzes, and controls potential threats to services and objectives. It encompasses risk assessment, mitigation strategies, and ongoing monitoring. ITIL 4 emphasizes proactive risk handling across all service management practices.
questions.justification_wrong: Problem Management focuses on root causes of incidents, Security Management addresses information protection, and Supplier Management handles vendor relationships. These are distinct from comprehensive risk identification and mitigation.

questions.text: In ITIL 4 Configuration Management, what is the primary purpose of maintaining a Configuration Management Database (CMDB)?
options: D. To document all approved changes and their implementation schedules
C. To track supplier performance metrics and contracts
A. To record all incidents and their resolution times
B. To provide a single source of truth for all configuration items and their relationships
questions.justification_correct: The CMDB maintains accurate information about all configuration items, their attributes, and relationships. It serves as the authoritative source for infrastructure and service components, supporting multiple ITIL practices. Per ITIL 4, it ensures visibility and control.
questions.justification_wrong: Incident tracking belongs to Incident Management, supplier data to Supplier Management, and change documentation to Change Management. The CMDB's core function is maintaining configuration integrity and relationships.

questions.text: Which ITIL 4 practice is responsible for planning and coordinating human and material resources to deliver one or more outcomes as specified in a project charter?
options: A. Release Management
B. Project Management
C. Service Transition
D. Portfolio Management
questions.justification_correct: Project Management coordinates resources and activities to deliver defined outcomes within specified parameters. ITIL 4 recognizes it as essential for managing temporary initiatives distinct from service delivery operations.
questions.justification_wrong: Release Management focuses on deploying authorized changes, Service Transition manages transition activities, and Portfolio Management aligns projects with strategy. Only Project Management directly manages temporary resource coordination for specific outcomes.

questions.text: In ITIL 4 Supplier Management, which approach best represents the practice's primary objective?
options: A. Maintaining competitive pricing by regularly changing vendors
B. Ensuring suppliers deliver agreed value, maintain quality standards, and reduce supply chain risks
C. Documenting all supplier contracts without performance monitoring
D. Minimizing the number of suppliers regardless of service quality
questions.justification_correct: Supplier Management ensures value delivery, maintains service quality, and mitigates risks through relationships with external providers. ITIL 4 emphasizes collaborative partnerships aligned with organizational objectives and service requirements.
questions.justification_wrong: Frequent vendor changes, unmonitored contracts, and supplier reduction ignore quality and risk management. Effective supplier management requires strategic relationships, performance oversight, and mutual value creation.

questions.text: In ITIL 4, which practice is primarily responsible for ensuring that IT services are delivered in accordance with agreed service levels and business expectations?
options: D. Asset Management
B. Incident Management
C. Change Management
A. Service Level Management
questions.justification_correct: Service Level Management establishes and monitors service level agreements (SLAs), ensuring IT services meet business requirements and customer expectations through defined metrics and reporting mechanisms.
questions.justification_wrong: Incident Management responds to service disruptions but doesn't establish service expectations. Change Management controls modifications, not service delivery monitoring. Asset Management tracks resources without performance oversight.

questions.text: According to ITIL 4 framework, what is the primary objective of the Service Request Management practice within the operational support domain?
options: D. To document and track all changes to the IT infrastructure
A. To handle pre-approved service requests and fulfill simple customer requests efficiently
B. To investigate and resolve all incidents without escalation procedures
C. To establish and maintain service level agreements with external suppliers
questions.justification_correct: Service Request Management handles pre-approved, low-risk requests (e.g., password resets, software licenses) through streamlined fulfillment processes, improving customer satisfaction and reducing operational overhead.
questions.justification_wrong: Incident Management addresses unplanned disruptions. Supplier Management handles external vendor relationships. Change Management controls infrastructure modifications, not service request fulfillment.

questions.text: In ITIL 4 Risk Management practice, which of the following best describes the relationship between risk tolerance and risk acceptance?
options: D. Risk acceptance must always exceed risk tolerance to ensure organizational resilience
A. Risk tolerance defines the organization's capacity to handle risk exposure, while risk acceptance is the deliberate decision to accept specific identified risks
B. Risk tolerance and risk acceptance are identical concepts used interchangeably in governance frameworks
C. Risk tolerance applies only to financial risks, while risk acceptance applies to operational risks
questions.justification_correct: Risk tolerance establishes organizational appetite/threshold for risk exposure; risk acceptance is the strategic decision to permit specific residual risks post-mitigation, enabling informed business continuity planning.
questions.justification_wrong: These are distinct concepts with different purposes. Risk tolerance applies across all risk categories, not just financial. Risk acceptance shouldn't exceed tolerance; it operates within established thresholds per governance standards.

questions.text: Which statement best reflects the ITIL 4 perspective on the relationship between Configuration Management and Change Management practices?
options: A. Configuration Management provides the baseline and change record data that Change Management uses to assess impact and ensure traceability of modifications
C. Configuration Management and Change Management are sequential processes where one must complete before the other begins
B. Change Management supersedes Configuration Management and renders it unnecessary in modern cloud environments
D. These practices operate independently with no operational interdependencies in ITIL 4 implementations
questions.justification_correct: Configuration Management maintains authoritative CMDB records enabling Change Management to perform impact analysis, track change history, and validate post-implementation baselines, ensuring service integrity throughout lifecycle.
questions.justification_wrong: Both practices remain essential and complementary. They operate concurrently, not sequentially. Cloud adoption doesn't eliminate configuration governance. Their integration is fundamental to change control effectiveness.

questions.text: According to ITIL 4, what is the key distinction between Information Security Management and Supplier Management when addressing third-party risks?
questions.tag: General Management Practices - Security and Suppliers
options: B. Information Security Management handles only internal threats, while Supplier Management addresses all external risks comprehensively
D. Both practices are identical and organizations should implement only one to avoid duplication

A. Information Security Management focuses on confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets, while Supplier Management ensures contractual compliance and service quality from external vendors
C. Supplier Management is responsible for all security obligations, eliminating the need for separate information security governance
questions.justification_correct: Information Security Management protects information systems and data integrity; Supplier Management governs vendor relationships, contracts, and performance standards. Both address third-party risks through complementary governance domains.
questions.justification_wrong: Security threats are internal and external. Supplier Management doesn't encompass all security responsibilities—they operate in distinct but integrated governance domains. Both practices are essential and non-redundant.

questions.text: An organization is implementing ITIL 4 Problem Management and needs to determine when to escalate a problem to Known Error status. According to ITIL 4 guidelines, which condition BEST triggers this transition?
options: A. When a workaround has been identified and documented, even if the root cause remains unknown
B. Only after the root cause has been completely eliminated from the production environment
D. Immediately upon problem detection, before any investigation is initiated
C. When the problem has affected more than 50% of the user base across all business units
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Problem Management establishes Known Error status when a workaround is identified and documented, enabling faster incident resolution. Root cause elimination is desirable but not mandatory for Known Error classification. This aligns with the service value chain's focus on efficiency.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B incorrectly requires complete root cause elimination; Known Errors exist specifically to handle problems with documented workarounds pending permanent fixes. Option C uses arbitrary metrics not aligned with ITIL principles. Option D contradicts Problem Management's investigation phase.

questions.text: During a Change Advisory Board (CAB) meeting, a standard change request for routine server patching is submitted. What should be the PRIMARY consideration for the CAB's decision according to ITIL 4 Change Management?
questions.tag: Change Management - Standard vs Normal Changes
options: C. All standard changes require full CAB approval due to their frequency and potential cumulative impact
A. Standard changes bypass CAB review entirely and proceed directly to implementation without additional authorization
D. Standard changes are approved by the Service Owner alone, without involving the CAB
B. The CAB must evaluate whether pre-approved change models and risk assessments still apply to the current context
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 defines standard changes as pre-approved with established models and acceptance criteria. CAB's role is validating these models remain appropriate and risk-appropriate within current operational context. Regular re-evaluation prevents assumption drift and ensures continued suitability.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A oversimplifies; standard changes follow streamlined processes but maintain governance oversight. Option C contradicts ITIL's efficiency principle for pre-approved changes. Option D incorrectly removes CAB from governance responsibility for standard changes.

questions.text: A Service Provider is designing Configuration Management Database (CMDB) scope for a complex multi-vendor environment. Which approach BEST aligns with ITIL 4 Configuration Management principles?
options: C. Include configuration items that directly support valued business outcomes and enable effective change/incident management
A. Include every hardware component, software license, and documentation item to achieve 100% coverage
D. Include all assets mentioned in vendor contracts, regardless of operational relevance
B. Include only business-critical assets and services essential to service delivery value
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 advocates defining CMDB scope around business value and operational necessity. Pragmatic scope focusing on items essential to incident resolution, change evaluation, and service continuity ensures maintainability and accuracy without overwhelming administrative burden.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A creates unmaintainable CMDB bloat reducing data quality. Option B excludes supporting infrastructure needed for incident/change management. Option D prioritizes contractual requirements over operational reality, creating irrelevant configurations.

questions.text: In Information Security Management, an organization discovers that a critical control has failed unexpectedly. According to ITIL 4, what should be the immediate next action within the service value chain?
questions.tag: Information Security Management - Control Failure Response
options: C. Wait for the next audit cycle to formally document and address the control failure
A. Immediately terminate all services affected by the control failure until replacement controls are implemented
D. Replace the failed control with an identical control from the same vendor to maintain consistency
B. Assess residual risk, implement interim compensating controls, and notify stakeholders while planning permanent remediation
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Information Security Management emphasizes risk-based response. Control failures require immediate risk assessment, interim mitigation through compensating controls, stakeholder communication, and planned remediation—balancing service continuity with security posture.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A causes unnecessary service disruption without risk assessment. Option C violates timely response principles and audit standards. Option D assumes identical replacement adequacy without addressing the root failure cause or risk context.

questions.text: A Supplier Management initiative requires evaluation of strategic vendor performance across multiple service delivery dimensions. Which metric framework BEST supports ITIL 4 Supplier Management objectives?
options: B. Balanced scorecard including quality, responsiveness, financial stability, and service delivery alignment with business outcomes
C. Contractual SLA compliance percentage, measured solely against agreed technical metrics
A. Cost reduction percentage alone, as this directly maximizes organizational budget efficiency
D. Supplier employee turnover rates and organizational stability indicators exclusively
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Supplier Management advocates comprehensive performance evaluation balancing quality, delivery, responsiveness, and strategic alignment. Balanced scorecard approach ensures suppliers support value creation across dimensions beyond cost, enabling effective strategic partnerships.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A reduces supplier evaluation to cost, ignoring quality and delivery impact on service value. Option C focuses narrowly on technical compliance without broader business outcome alignment. Option D measures operational stability without assessing actual service delivery performance.

questions.text: In ITIL 4 Problem Management, which approach best balances reactive problem solving with proactive problem identification to minimize incident recurrence?
questions.tag: Problem Management - Reactive vs Proactive Strategy
options: B. Focusing exclusively on high-impact incidents without analyzing patterns across lower-priority tickets
C. Delegating all problem investigation to external vendors without internal knowledge retention
A. Implementing a systematic trend analysis process that correlates incident data with configuration items to identify root causes and prevent future occurrences
D. Creating separate teams for incident and problem management with minimal communication channels
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Problem Management emphasizes both reactive (fixing known errors) and proactive (preventing future incidents) approaches. Systematic trend analysis using incident correlation enables organizations to identify patterns early, reducing recurrence rates and improving service quality through data-driven root cause analysis.
questions.justification_wrong: Ignoring lower-priority incidents, outsourcing without retention, or silos between teams violates ITIL 4 integration principles and prevents the holistic view necessary for effective problem identification and prevention.

questions.text: According to ITIL 4 Change Management, what is the primary purpose of implementing a Change Advisory Board (CAB) with appropriate stakeholder representation?
questions.tag: Change Management - CAB Structure and Governance
options: C. To eliminate the need for post-implementation reviews and testing procedures
D. To assign blame when changes fail without implementing preventive measures
B. To ensure changes are evaluated from multiple perspectives, balancing risk, business impact, and technical feasibility while maintaining stakeholder buy-in
A. To reduce the number of changes by rejecting most change requests before assessment
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 establishes CAB as a collaborative governance body ensuring changes receive multidisciplinary assessment. Cross-functional representation (IT, business, operations) reduces blind spots, improves decision quality, and builds organizational consensus. This aligns with the guiding principle of 'collaborate and promote visibility.'
questions.justification_wrong: CAB is not a rejection gate or blame mechanism. Rejecting changes indiscriminately stifles innovation. CAB complements, not replaces, testing. Post-implementation reviews remain essential for continuous improvement and learning.

questions.text: In ITIL 4 IT Asset Management, how should organizations reconcile conflicts between financial asset depreciation schedules and technical asset lifecycle management to optimize total cost of ownership (TCO)?
questions.tag: IT Asset Management - Financial vs Technical Lifecycle Alignment
options: D. Maintain completely separate financial and technical asset registers with no cross-referencing or reconciliation
A. Align depreciation schedules exclusively with manufacturer specifications, ignoring financial accounting requirements
C. Retain assets beyond technical end-of-life solely to maximize depreciation benefits without regard to operational costs
B. Establish integrated asset governance where finance and IT collaborate to synchronize depreciation cycles with maintenance costs, replacement timelines, and business value delivery
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Asset Management requires integration between financial and operational perspectives. Collaborative alignment between finance and IT ensures depreciation reflects true asset value, maintenance costs are anticipated, and replacement decisions minimize TCO. This supports the 'holistic approach' principle essential for organizational value.
questions.justification_wrong: Ignoring financial requirements violates compliance. Over-retaining assets inflates operational costs. Separated registers create data inconsistencies and prevent informed decision-making, contradicting ITIL's integration and value focus.

questions.text: Which strategy best demonstrates ITIL 4 compliance when managing supplier relationships through the Supplier Management practice, particularly for critical service dependencies?
questions.tag: Supplier Management - Critical Dependency Risk Mitigation
options: B. Implementing a tiered supplier portfolio with primary and backup suppliers for critical services, supported by SLAs, performance metrics, and regular supplier reviews
C. Minimizing supplier interactions to reduce communication overhead and cost
D. Allowing suppliers to determine their own performance standards without organizational input
A. Establishing single-supplier contracts to simplify management, even for mission-critical services
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Supplier Management emphasizes supplier performance optimization and risk mitigation. Multi-tier supplier strategies for critical dependencies reduce single points of failure. SLAs with defined metrics and regular reviews enable proactive management, ensuring service continuity and value delivery aligned with organizational strategy.
questions.justification_wrong: Single suppliers create concentration risk. Minimal interaction prevents performance optimization. Supplier-defined standards without contractual alignment create uncontrolled variability, violating governance principles and risking service disruption.

questions.text: In ITIL 4 Release Management and Deployment Management integration, how should organizations balance automation velocity with quality assurance to achieve continuous delivery while maintaining service stability?
questions.tag: Release & Deployment Management - Automation vs Quality Trade-offs
options: A. Automating all deployment steps without testing, prioritizing speed over reliability to maximize release frequency
D. Deploying directly to production without staging environments or pre-deployment validation procedures
B. Implementing automated testing pipelines with progressive deployment patterns (canary/blue-green) combined with automated rollback capabilities and quality gates
C. Reverting to entirely manual deployment processes to ensure maximum human oversight and eliminate automation risks
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 integrates Release and Deployment Management through intelligent automation. Automated testing pipelines, progressive deployment strategies, and automated rollback mechanisms enable rapid, reliable releases. Quality gates ensure standards compliance. This approach balances velocity with stability, supporting continuous delivery principles.
questions.justification_wrong: Skipping tests increases defect risk. Pure manual processes prevent continuous delivery velocity. Direct production deployment without validation causes service disruptions. These approaches contradict ITIL's focus on reliability and customer value.

questions.text: In ITIL 4, when establishing a supplier management practice, which approach best balances cost optimization with service quality assurance across multiple vendors?
options: D. Establish annual renegotiations regardless of supplier performance to maintain competitive pricing
C. Consolidate all services to a single supplier to streamline management and reduce overhead costs
B. Negotiate identical contracts with all suppliers to ensure consistency and eliminate favoritism
A. Implement tiered supplier categorization with differentiated SLAs and performance metrics aligned to business criticality
questions.justification_correct: Tiered supplier categorization enables risk mitigation, cost optimization, and quality assurance simultaneously. ITIL 4 Supplier Management practice emphasizes differentiated approaches based on strategic importance and risk profiles, ensuring alignment with organizational objectives.
questions.justification_wrong: Identical contracts ignore criticality differences; single-vendor strategies increase dependency risk; annual renegotiations without performance triggers violate ITIL 4's performance-driven governance principles.

questions.text: During a major organizational transformation, the Change Management practice must balance innovation velocity with stability. Which control mechanism is most effective in ITIL 4?
options: C. Require identical testing procedures for all changes regardless of complexity or impact assessment
D. Delegate all change decisions to development teams to accelerate time-to-market
B. Suspend all change approvals during transformation to maximize speed of implementation
A. Implement parallel change advisory boards with expedited approval pathways for low-risk changes and comprehensive testing for high-risk changes
questions.justification_correct: Risk-stratified approval processes enable controlled velocity. ITIL 4 Change Management emphasizes proportionate governance—expedited pathways for low-risk changes while maintaining rigorous controls for high-impact changes, balancing innovation and stability.
questions.justification_wrong: Suspending approvals violates governance; identical procedures waste resources on low-risk changes; delegating to development teams bypasses necessary cross-functional oversight required by ITIL 4 change governance.

questions.text: In IT Asset Management, an organization discovers significant undocumented hardware assets during a compliance audit. What is the most effective remediation strategy aligned with ITIL 4 principles?
questions.tag: IT Asset Management - Compliance and Discovery
options: B. Document assets manually in spreadsheets and perform annual physical audits
D. Establish shadow IT asset registers separate from official records
C. Ignore undocumented assets if they are not causing operational issues
A. Immediately retire all undocumented assets and implement automated discovery tools with configuration management integration to prevent recurrence
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Asset Management integrates automated discovery with Configuration Management Database (CMDB) synchronization. This ensures continuous compliance, prevents shadow assets, and enables accurate lifecycle management and risk assessment.
questions.justification_wrong: Manual documentation lacks scalability; ignoring undocumented assets violates compliance requirements; shadow registers create governance gaps; deferred action perpetuates compliance risk.

questions.text: An organization experiences recurring critical incidents despite frequent problem-solving efforts. According to ITIL 4 Problem Management, what indicates systemic practice failure?
questions.tag: Problem Management - Root Cause Analysis Effectiveness
options: B. The organization conducts monthly problem reviews regardless of incident volume trends
A. Problem records lack documented relationship mappings to configuration items, changes, and incidents, preventing pattern recognition and preventing knowledge reuse
C. Problem managers report directly to the IT Director without escalation protocols
D. Root cause analyses are completed within 48 hours of incident closure
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Problem Management emphasizes integrated analysis across incidents, configurations, and changes. Missing relationship mappings prevent pattern detection, root cause identification, and proactive improvement—causing recurring incidents.
questions.justification_wrong: Fixed review cadences ignore trends; reporting structures vary organizationally; rapid RCA completion is efficient. Only missing traceability systematically prevents knowledge reuse and pattern recognition.

questions.text: When implementing Security Management across multiple cloud platforms, which governance control best ensures compliance with ITIL 4 principles while addressing cloud-specific risks?
options: D. Implement separate security teams for each cloud platform to ensure specialized expertise
A. Establish platform-agnostic security baselines with cloud-provider-specific control mappings, continuous compliance monitoring, and incident correlation across platforms
B. Apply identical on-premises security policies directly to all cloud environments without modification
C. Delegate security responsibility entirely to cloud providers through contractual agreements
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Security Management requires unified governance frameworks adapted to environmental differences. Platform-agnostic baselines with provider-specific mappings enable consistent risk management while respecting cloud architecture variations.
questions.justification_wrong: Direct policy replication ignores cloud-native architecture differences; delegating responsibility eliminates organizational oversight; separate teams fragment governance and impede correlation analysis.

questions.text: An organization implements IT Asset Management integrated with Configuration Management Database (CMDB). During a major infrastructure change, assets are decommissioned but not properly updated in the CMDB. Which practice's control objective is primarily violated, and what is the cascading risk?
questions.tag: IT Asset Management & Configuration Management Integration
options: C. Service Design's control objective to optimize service delivery; risk only affects future planning cycles
B. Change Management's control objective to prevent unauthorized changes; risk is limited to financial reporting inaccuracies
D. Problem Management's control objective to resolve root causes; risk impacts only incident resolution timelines
A. Asset Management's control objective to maintain accurate asset records and inventory; cascading risk includes inaccurate change impact analysis and compliance violations
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Asset Management practice requires accurate asset lifecycle tracking. CMDB synchronization ensures Change Management, Release Management, and compliance processes operate on reliable data. Decommissioned assets create configuration drift, affecting change impact assessments and audit trails per ISO/IEC 20000-1.
questions.justification_wrong: Options B, C, D misalign with asset management's primary control scope. Change Management doesn't own asset decommissioning oversight; Problem Management addresses symptoms, not asset record governance; Service Design operates at strategic rather than operational control levels.

questions.text: A Supplier Management practice identifies a critical third-party cloud provider with single points of failure in SLA delivery. The organization must balance cost optimization with risk mitigation. Which strategy best aligns with ITIL 4 principles while maintaining governance?
options: C. Accept the risk and document it in the risk register without contractual modifications to preserve cost benefits
D. Require the supplier to achieve 99.99% uptime without modifying contractual terms or establishing backup arrangements
B. Immediately switch to multiple suppliers regardless of cost to eliminate all single-point-of-failure risks
A. Implement contractual redundancy clauses, establish independent monitoring, conduct quarterly business reviews with risk-based KPIs, and maintain contingency supplier relationships
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Supplier Management integrates with Risk Management practice. Risk-based approach requires proportionate controls: contractual safeguards, monitoring mechanisms, and contingency planning. Quarterly reviews enable adaptive governance. Aligns with ISO/IEC 20000-1 supplier governance requirements and balanced risk appetite.
questions.justification_wrong: B ignores cost-benefit trade-offs; C violates Risk Management obligations; D assumes contractual enforcement without structural mitigation, creating residual risk exposure without governance mechanism.

questions.text: During a Deployment Management activity for a critical production service, a configuration drift is detected between the deployment pipeline artifact and the live infrastructure. The deployment was approved and released. What is the correct remediation sequence?
options: D. Escalate to Problem Management for root cause analysis while maintaining current state without remediation
B. Roll back to previous version immediately without investigation to minimize service disruption
A. Halt deployment validation, immediately initiate a Change Advisory Board (CAB) review, verify CMDB accuracy, implement corrective action, and update deployment processes to prevent recurrence
C. Continue monitoring without action since the deployment was approved; update CMDB post-facto to match infrastructure
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Deployment Management requires configuration accuracy validation pre and post-deployment. Drift detection requires governance restoration: CAB review ensures control, CMDB synchronization maintains single source of truth, corrective action addresses immediate risk, process improvement prevents recurrence per ISO/IEC 20000-1 change control.
questions.justification_wrong: B skips root cause investigation; C violates configuration control principles; D defers governance action, allowing drift to persist and affecting future changes and incident diagnosis.

questions.text: An organization's Information Security Management practice identifies that critical infrastructure assets managed by Infrastructure and Platform Management lack encryption key rotation controls. Audit findings mandate remediation. Which governance model ensures accountability and sustainable control?
questions.tag: Information Security Management & Infrastructure Management Integration
options: A. Establish shared accountability matrix defining Security and Infrastructure responsibilities, implement automated key rotation via Infrastructure tooling, create joint KPIs, and conduct monthly control effectiveness reviews
D. Document the audit finding in the risk register and defer implementation until next budget cycle
C. Transfer all encryption controls to Infrastructure Management as technical owners without ongoing security oversight
B. Assign full responsibility to Information Security Management to implement and operate all encryption controls independently
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 emphasizes integrated practice operation. Security and Infrastructure Management share accountability: Security defines requirements, Infrastructure implements technical controls. Automation ensures consistency, joint KPIs align incentives, regular reviews enable adaptive governance per ISO/IEC 27001 compliance integration requirements.
questions.justification_wrong: B creates silos preventing technical feasibility; C removes security oversight, increasing residual risk; D defers compliance obligations and audit remediation timelines, violating regulatory requirements.

questions.text: A Risk Management practice assessment reveals that operational risks from Service Desk tool failures are not formally tracked or escalated. The organization uses Service Desk as the incident entry point for all IT services. What is the critical control gap and required remediation?
questions.tag: Risk Management & Service Desk Practice Integration
options: C. Incident Management process is inefficient; optimize incident categorization workflows to reduce Service Desk processing load
B. Service Desk tool lacks advanced analytics; implement machine learning to predict incidents rather than addressing availability risks

A. Lack of Service Desk resilience risk in enterprise risk register; remediate by conducting Service Desk criticality assessment, establishing RPO/RTO targets, implementing redundancy, monitoring uptime KPIs, and including in business continuity plans
D. Service Desk technicians lack training; conduct technical certification programs to prevent tool failures
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Risk Management requires identifying critical dependencies and single points of failure. Service Desk as incident entry point creates operational risk exposure. Remediation: criticality assessment establishes risk level, RPO/RTO defines recovery requirements, redundancy mitigates failure impact, KPIs monitor control effectiveness per ISO/IEC 20000-1 operational resilience.
questions.justification_wrong: B addresses performance optimization, not availability risk; C optimizes workflow efficiency without addressing infrastructure resilience; D focuses on skill development rather than systemic availability controls.

questions.text: An organization implements a Configuration Management System (CMS) to track IT assets across multiple data centers. During a major incident, the CMS data is found to be 60% inaccurate. Which practice should be prioritized to remediate this situation?
options: B. Increase the number of Service Desk agents to manually verify each configuration item
A. Implement a comprehensive IT Asset Management practice with regular reconciliation cycles and automated discovery tools
D. Reduce the scope of the CMS to only critical assets to simplify management
C. Outsource all asset tracking to a third-party supplier using managed services
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 emphasizes IT Asset Management as foundational to Configuration Management. Regular reconciliation, automated discovery, and data quality controls are normative practices per ITIL Service Transition guidelines. This ensures accuracy and supports incident resolution effectively.
questions.justification_wrong: Manual verification (B) is resource-intensive and non-scalable. Outsourcing (C) transfers responsibility but doesn't solve root causes. Scope reduction (D) compromises visibility required for effective service management and incident response.

questions.text: During a change implementation, a junior analyst bypasses the formal change process due to time pressure, causing a production outage. Which combination of practices should the organization strengthen to prevent recurrence?
options: A. Enforce change management discipline through governance controls and implement risk assessment mandates for all changes
D. Assign accountability to individual team members without formal process oversight
C. Implement longer change windows to reduce implementation pressure
B. Require Service Desk approval for all technical changes regardless of complexity
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Change Management practice requires formal governance and risk assessment integration. Mandatory risk evaluation (per Risk Management practice) combined with enforced process discipline prevents unauthorized changes. This aligns with normative control frameworks and CAB oversight principles.
questions.justification_wrong: Service Desk approval (B) lacks technical expertise for risk assessment. Longer windows (C) address symptoms, not root cause of process bypass. Individual accountability (D) without formal controls enables future non-compliance and bypasses governance requirements.

questions.text: A supplier providing critical infrastructure services has a single point of failure in their delivery model. Which Information Security Management and Supplier Management practices should be applied together?
options: D. Transition immediately to a different supplier without performance assessment
C. Accept the risk and maintain high insurance coverage instead of addressing supplier capability
B. Require the supplier to implement their own ISMS certification without organizational oversight
A. Establish contractual redundancy requirements, perform supply chain risk assessments, and enforce SLA penalties for downtime
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Supplier Management mandates supply chain risk assessments and contractual clarity. Information Security Management requires embedded security controls in supplier agreements. Combined governance ensures redundancy, continuity, and risk mitigation per ISO/IEC 27001 principles.
questions.justification_wrong: Self-certification without oversight (B) removes organizational control. Risk acceptance (C) violates Risk Management principles. Immediate transition (D) lacks due diligence and ignores assessment-based decision making required by Supplier Management practice.

questions.text: An organization experiences recurring critical incidents due to inadequate knowledge transfer during infrastructure platform changes. Which practices should be integrated to resolve this?
options: A. Integrate Deployment Management with Infrastructure & Platform Management to include post-deployment knowledge transfer, documentation, and operational runbook validation
B. Implement mandatory on-call rotations immediately after deployment without structured training
C. Rely solely on vendor documentation without organizational knowledge capture
D. Delay operational handover until all incidents are resolved in the testing environment
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Deployment Management requires knowledge transfer gates. Infrastructure & Platform Management practice mandates operational readiness including documented runbooks and trained staff. Integration ensures continuity and incident prevention per service transition best practices.
questions.justification_wrong: On-call rotation (B) without training creates operational risk. Vendor documentation alone (C) doesn't capture organizational context. Indefinite testing (D) delays value realization and operational maturity, violating deployment closure requirements.

questions.text: During a problem investigation, the Problem Manager identifies that 40% of incidents stem from outdated information in the Known Error Database. What should be the primary corrective action?
options: A. Establish a periodic KED review cycle integrated with incident analysis to validate and retire obsolete entries, linking resolution to incident closure processes
C. Increase the Problem Manager's team size to manage database entries alongside incident handling
B. Archive the entire KED and require analysts to research each incident independently without reference materials
D. Implement automated problem detection tools to bypass manual database lookups
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Problem Management practice requires KED maintenance as a core responsibility. Periodic review cycles tied to incident closure ensure currency and accuracy. This reflects normative knowledge management governance and supports faster incident resolution through reliable diagnostics.
questions.justification_wrong: Archive approach (B) loses institutional knowledge and increases MTTR. Team expansion (C) addresses symptom without fixing root cause. Automation tools (D) still require accurate underlying data; they don't eliminate database quality issues.

questions.text: An organization implements ITIL 4 practices and experiences frequent service interruptions due to inadequate change coordination. Which combination of practices should be prioritized to establish a robust control framework?
options: D. Risk Management without integration to change workflows
C. Incident Management focused on rapid resolution post-deployment
B. Service Request Management alone with automated approval workflows
A. Change Management with Configuration Management providing baseline visibility and impact analysis before approvals
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 emphasizes bidirectional integration. Change Management requires Configuration Management's CMDB accuracy for impact assessment. This combination enables informed change decisions, reducing unintended consequences and service disruptions through proper dependency mapping.
questions.justification_wrong: Service Request Management handles customer requests, not change control. Incident Management reacts post-failure. Risk Management alone lacks operational change controls. These miss the core relationship required for preventive change governance.

questions.text: During a supplier evaluation for critical infrastructure services, you must assess compliance with organizational security requirements and contract performance metrics. Which supplier management approach best aligns with ITIL 4 principles?
options: B. Conduct initial security certification only at contract signature
D. Focus exclusively on cost reduction without security reassessment
C. Delegate all compliance verification to the supplier's internal audit team
A. Implement continuous performance monitoring with documented security assessments, contractual KPIs, and regular audit schedules aligned to risk profiles
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Supplier Management emphasizes ongoing relationship management with integrated metrics. Continuous monitoring, documented assessments, and risk-based audit schedules ensure security posture maintenance and contractual compliance throughout the engagement lifecycle.
questions.justification_wrong: One-time certification creates compliance gaps. Delegating verification abdicates responsibility. Cost-only focus ignores security risks. These violate ITIL 4's principle of integrated governance and shared responsibility in supplier relationships.

questions.text: A technology organization experiences recurring problems with infrastructure platform stability. Root cause analysis reveals systemic configuration drift and inadequate deployment discipline. Which practice combination addresses this at the managing professional level?
questions.tag: Infrastructure & Platform Management with Deployment Management
options: D. Implement more frequent manual configuration reviews
B. Increase monitoring alert sensitivity to catch failures earlier
A. Implement Infrastructure & Platform Management with rigorous version control, Infrastructure as Code, and Deployment Management automation to enforce consistency
C. Expand the incident resolution team for faster response
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 managing professional level requires systemic controls. Infrastructure as Code with Deployment Management automation enforces configuration consistency, eliminates drift, and provides auditability. This addresses root causes rather than symptoms, enabling reliable platform operations.
questions.justification_wrong: Monitoring detects problems after occurrence. Expanding incident teams doesn't prevent systemic issues. Manual reviews remain error-prone and unscalable. These are reactive measures that don't address configuration drift's root engineering causes.

questions.text: An organization must establish risk governance across IT service delivery. Which risk management practice implementation approach best ensures alignment with ITIL 4's value co-creation model?
options: D. Implement risk management independent of other practices with separate reporting
A. Establish risk identification and assessment cycles integrated with change, supplier, and security practices; communicate risk visibility to business stakeholders and decision-makers
C. Focus risk management only on infrastructure technical failures
B. Maintain risk registers exclusively within IT operations without business visibility
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4's value co-creation requires stakeholder engagement. Risk Management integrated across change, supplier, and security practices with transparent business communication ensures shared understanding of service risks and enables informed trade-off decisions.
questions.justification_wrong: Siloed IT risk registers prevent business alignment. Technical-only focus misses business continuity and compliance risks. Independent implementation creates fragmented governance. These violate ITIL 4's integrated approach and stakeholder value principles.

questions.text: During service asset and configuration management implementation, inconsistencies emerge between asset inventory records and actual deployed infrastructure. What integrated approach resolves this discrepancy while establishing ongoing accuracy?
options: A. Conduct discovery audit reconciliation; implement single authoritative CMDB with Deployment Management integration for automated updates; establish governance for change-driven updates
B. Perform one-time manual inventory count and accept ongoing drift
D. Rely on supplier-provided asset lists without internal validation
C. Maintain separate asset and configuration databases with periodic manual synchronization
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 managing professional level requires integrated data governance. Discovery audits establish baseline accuracy. Single CMDB with Deployment Management automation ensures real-time updates. Change-driven governance prevents future drift through systematic controls.
questions.justification_wrong: Manual counts create transient accuracy without control mechanisms. Separate databases perpetuate inconsistencies. Supplier-only data lacks organizational accountability and excludes internal assets. These lack ITIL 4's emphasis on integrated, automated governance.

questions.text: In ITIL 4, when establishing a supplier management practice, which approach best ensures alignment with organizational strategy while maintaining operational flexibility?
options: A. Implement tiered supplier categorization based on criticality and business impact, with differentiated governance models for each tier
B. Standardize all supplier contracts with identical terms regardless of service criticality
D. Establish supplier relationships primarily through informal agreements to maintain flexibility
C. Assign a single supplier for each service category to reduce management complexity
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 SVS emphasizes segmenting suppliers by strategic importance, applying risk-based management. Tiered categorization enables appropriate governance, contract complexity, and performance expectations aligned with business impact and organizational strategy.
questions.justification_wrong: Standardized contracts ignore criticality differences, single sourcing creates dependency risks, and informal agreements lack accountability mechanisms required for enterprise supplier governance.

questions.text: An organization implements information security management across its service portfolio. Which practice integration is most critical for detecting unauthorized configuration changes that may introduce security vulnerabilities?
options: C. Rely solely on incident management to identify security breaches after they occur
B. Implement security scanning tools independently without coordinating with configuration management
A. Integrate security management with change management and configuration management to establish a baseline and detect deviations
D. Establish security governance separate from technical practices to maintain independence
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 mandates integrated security practices. Configuration management maintains CMDB baselines; change management controls modifications; security management monitors alignment. Integration enables preventive detection of unauthorized changes affecting security posture.
questions.justification_wrong: Independent scanning lacks context; reactive incident approach creates security gaps; separation prevents holistic visibility required for enterprise security governance.

questions.text: During a complex infrastructure deployment, the deployment management practice must coordinate with other practices. Which combination is essential for managing deployment risks and ensuring rollback capability?
options: A. Coordinate with change management for approval, release management for packaging, and service asset & configuration management for baseline documentation
C. Manage deployments independently from other practices to maintain autonomy
B. Execute deployments without change management approval to accelerate delivery
D. Rely only on incident management to handle deployment failures reactively
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 requires deployment practice coordination: change management authorizes; release management ensures controlled packaging; service asset & configuration management maintains baseline records enabling rollback and impact analysis pre-deployment.
questions.justification_wrong: Bypassing change management creates governance gaps; independence prevents coordinated risk control; reactive-only incident management leaves no rollback foundation.

questions.text: An organization experiences recurring incidents from undocumented service dependencies. Which problem management approach best addresses the root cause while preventing recurrence?
options: B. Immediately create incident tickets for each occurrence without analyzing underlying causes
D. Implement workarounds to temporarily resolve incidents without investigating root causes
A. Conduct root cause analysis using five-why technique, integrate findings with configuration management to document dependencies, and implement preventive controls
C. Assign blame to individuals involved rather than investigating systemic issues
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 problem management emphasizes structured RCA, linking findings to CMDB for persistent documentation. Integration with configuration management creates institutional knowledge preventing recurrence through documented dependencies.
questions.justification_wrong: Ticket-only approach perpetuates incidents; blame assignment ignores systemic problems; workarounds without RCA create recurring issues and technical debt.

questions.text: In implementing risk management practice across the service portfolio, how should inherent and residual risk be properly managed according to ITIL 4 principles?
options: A. Identify inherent risks, apply mitigating controls through other practices, and reassess to determine residual risk, accepting only tolerable residual risk
C. Treat all identified risks equally without differentiation by impact and probability
B. Focus only on residual risk without evaluating inherent risk levels
D. Avoid risk documentation to minimize administrative overhead
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 risk management requires evaluating inherent risk baseline, applying controls via integrated practices (security, change, supplier), reassessing for residual risk. Residual risk acceptance determines service viability and determines mitigation strategies.
questions.justification_wrong: Omitting inherent risk masks true exposure; undifferentiated treatment misallocates resources; undocumented risks create governance and compliance violations.

questions.text: An organization implements integrated risk management and supplier management practices. During a critical service delivery, a third-party supplier fails to meet SLA commitments due to inadequate risk mitigation controls. Which approach best addresses this systemic failure?
options: B. Immediately terminate the supplier contract and source from alternative vendors without risk assessment.
C. Document the failure and address it only during the next annual supplier performance review.
D. Transfer all operational risks to the supplier through increased SLA penalties.
A. Establish joint risk registers with suppliers, conduct quarterly risk reviews, and embed risk accountability in supplier contracts with escalation procedures.
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 emphasizes integrated risk and supplier management. Joint risk registers, collaborative reviews, and contractual risk accountability create accountability loops. This aligns with SVT (Service Value System) principles of coordinated practice implementation.
questions.justification_wrong: Reactive termination ignores systemic issues. Annual reviews miss escalation windows. Risk transfer without mitigation violates risk ownership principles and creates liability exposure under ITIL 4 governance.

questions.text: During IT asset management and configuration management implementation, conflicting data exists between the CMDB and the physical asset inventory across 50 datacenters. What is the optimal remediation strategy?
options: C. Manually re-enter all asset data into both systems without automated validation.

A. Conduct a phased reconciliation audit using authoritative sources, establish a single source of truth (SSOT), implement automated discovery tools, and create governance controls to prevent future divergence.
B. Accept the data discrepancy as normal and proceed with service delivery using CMDB data exclusively.
D. Replace the CMDB with the asset management system and discard historical records.
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 Configuration Management requires authoritative SSoT. Phased audits, automated discovery, and governance prevent data decay. This approach aligns with Continual Improvement Model (CIM) and reduces service delivery risks.
questions.justification_wrong: Accepting divergence violates configuration integrity. Manual re-entry introduces human error. Discarding records loses audit trails and compliance evidence required for security and financial governance.

questions.text: An organization experiences cascading incident escalations due to poor integration between incident management and problem management practices. Root cause analysis repeatedly identifies the same infrastructure failures. How should these practices be synthesized?
options: B. Keep incident and problem management completely separate to avoid confusion about ownership.

A. Implement a closed-loop integration: incidents trigger problem investigations when repeat patterns exceed thresholds; problem solutions automatically update incident response playbooks and configuration items.
C. Escalate all incidents to problem management regardless of recurrence patterns.
D. Use problem management only for post-incident reviews without proactive pattern detection.
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 mandates incident-problem synthesis through feedback loops. Threshold-based escalation, automated playbook updates, and CI synchronization create preventive capacity. This reduces Mean Time To Recover (MTTR) and incident volume.
questions.justification_wrong: Separation eliminates learning loops and perpetuates recurring failures. Indiscriminate escalation wastes resources. Retrospective-only problem management misses proactive intervention windows defined in ITIL practice guidance.

questions.text: During information security management practice deployment, the organization discovers that change management and deployment management lack integrated security validation controls. Unauthorized changes bypass security approvals. What is the correct remediation?
questions.tag: Information Security, Change & Deployment Management Integration
options: C. Implement security controls only after deployment is complete.

A. Embed mandatory security impact assessments in change CAB workflows, require security sign-off before deployment authorization, integrate vulnerability scanning in deployment pipelines, and audit security control effectiveness.
D. Allow deployment teams to self-authorize security-related changes without external validation.
B. Delay all changes until a dedicated security team can manually review each request.
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 integrates security across practices. Pre-deployment security assessment, CAB integration, automated scanning, and post-deployment audits create defense-in-depth. Normative: SVT Practice Model and Seven Guiding Principles (holistic approach).
questions.justification_wrong: Sequential manual review creates bottlenecks. Post-deployment controls miss attack surfaces. Self-authorization violates segregation-of-duties and creates compliance failures under ISO/IEC 27001 and regulatory frameworks.

questions.text: An organization struggles with the synthesis of project management, service transition (deployment), and supplier management when implementing a critical cloud platform. Multiple stakeholders report misaligned objectives and accountability gaps. Which integrated governance model resolves this?
options: D. Separate project delivery from supplier management and deployment into sequential, non-overlapping phases.
C. Assign complete project control to the supplier with minimal internal governance oversight.
B. Allow each practice (project, deployment, supplier) to operate independently with periodic status meetings.

A. Establish a unified governance framework: single project charter aligning all objectives, supplier responsibilities mapped to RACI matrices, deployment gates controlled by cross-functional steering committee, with integrated KPIs tracking project, service, and supplier performance.
questions.justification_correct: ITIL 4 SVT requires integrated governance. Unified charter, RACI clarity, gated delivery, and aligned KPIs eliminate silos. This reflects Guiding Principles: collaborative culture, integrated practice approach, and holistic service delivery.
questions.justification_wrong: Independent operations cause misalignment and rework. Supplier-centric control eliminates organizational accountability. Sequential phases create delays, reduce adaptability, and violate iterative delivery principles in modern ITIL practice.
